nuance: Do the action within a window before it ends — while it's still light, still warm, still available.
caution: うちに vs 間に: うちに wants a change-implying state (明るいうちに, before it stops being true), while 間に just marks a span; also, negative-verb うちに (忘れないうちに) means 'before it becomes X', not 'while not X'.

examples.en: I'll hang out the laundry while it's still light.
I'll write down the station's name before I forget.
While I'm still young, I want to travel a lot.

peg.ja: 若いうちに、世界を見ておきたい。
peg.romaji: Wakai uchi ni, sekai o mite okitai.
peg.en: While you're young, you should see the world.
peg.source: 葬送のフリーレン — Frieren (styled)

nuance: Each time X happens, Y follows — a recurring pairing.
caution: たびに always pairs each occurrence with a result, so it can't take a fixed frequency word like 毎日 (毎日たびに is wrong) — use it with an event verb, not a schedule.

examples.en: Every time I look at the Hokkaido photos, I want to go back.
Every time I go to the ward office, a different document is required.
Every time I hear this song, I remember Tokyo.

peg.ja: この海を見るたびに、仲間を思い出す。
peg.romaji: Kono umi o miru tabi ni, nakama o omoidasu.
peg.en: Every time I see this sea, I remember my crew.

nuance: The instant the first action finished, the second happened — often unexpected.
caution: たとたん needs the second clause to be a sudden, UNCONTROLLED result you notice — so it can't take a command, intention, or request (×開いたとたん、乗ろう); use それから/たら for planned follow-ups.

examples.en: The moment the train doors opened, people surged in.
The moment I stepped outside, it started to rain.
The moment I got into the futon, I fell fast asleep.

peg.ja: ドアを開けたとたん、敵が現れた。
peg.romaji: Doa o aketa totan, teki ga arawareta.
peg.en: The moment I opened the door, the enemy appeared.

connection: V-ている / N-の + 最中に
nuance: Interrupted right at the peak of doing something.
caution: 最中に needs an ongoing action at its peak, so it takes 〜ている or a noun (食事の最中に), never a plain V-dict; and the interruption should be a sudden intrusion, not a scheduled next step.

examples.en: The phone rang right in the middle of my job interview.
Right in the middle of cooking, the share house fire alarm went off.
The landlord showed up right in the middle of cleaning.

peg.ja: 戦いの最中に、気を抜くな。
peg.romaji: Tatakai no saichū ni, ki o nuku na.
peg.en: In the thick of battle, don't let your guard down.

nuance: After the last action, the expected follow-up never came.
caution: たきり must attach to the past (V-た) and the expected continuation never comes — don't confuse it with たきり meaning 'only' after a number; here the point is 'did X and then nothing since'.

examples.en: I haven't been in touch with my coworker since we met last month.
I bought the study book but haven't opened it even once since.
I haven't gone back even once since I left my parents' home.

peg.ja: あの日別れたきり、彼には会っていない。
peg.romaji: Ano hi wakareta kiri, kare ni wa atte inai.
peg.en: Since we parted that day, I haven't seen him.

meaning: for the first time in (a period)
nuance: After a long gap, doing the thing again — emphasises how much time passed.
caution: ぶり attaches to a length of time (三年ぶり), not to a verb, and it's about resuming after a gap — don't use it for a plain 'ago' (that's 前); '3年ぶりに会った' = met again after 3 years, not '3 years ago'.

examples.en: I returned to Japan for the first time in three years.
It cleared up for the first time in a week, so I aired out the futon.
I went to an onsen for the first time in two years.

peg.ja: 十年ぶりに、故郷の空を見上げた。
peg.romaji: Jūnen buri ni, kokyō no sora o miageta.
peg.en: For the first time in ten years, I looked up at my hometown sky.

meaning: while at it / taking the opportunity
connection: V-dict/V-た / N-の + ついでに
nuance: Do a secondary thing while already out doing the main one.
caution: ついでに means the second act is a bonus done while already doing the main one, so the main action must genuinely be happening — it's not a plain 'and also', and the two acts share one trip/occasion.

examples.en: While I'm out shopping, I'll drop off the package at the konbini.
While on my walk, I checked out the nearby park too.
On my way to the ward office, I stopped by the bank too.

peg.ja: 散歩のついでに、君を助けてやろう。
peg.romaji: Sanpo no tsuide ni, kimi o tasukete yarō.
peg.en: While I'm out for a walk, I'll help you out.

nuance: Sets up a hypothesis to reason from: assuming that were true, then...
caution: としたら sets up a hypothesis to reason from ('supposing X'), so pair it with a judgment/consequence — don't use it for a real, decided plan (use なら/たら for that).

examples.en: Supposing I move, a cheaper-rent area would be better.
If I assume I work weekends, does the hourly wage go up?
Supposing I extend my visa, what documents would I need?

peg.ja: もし世界を救えるとしたら、君はどうする？
peg.romaji: Moshi sekai o sukueru to shitara, kimi wa dō suru?
peg.en: Supposing you could save the world, what would you do?
peg.source: 魔法少女まどか☆マギカ — Kyubey (styled)

nuance: たとえ signals the coming ても — 'even supposing X, still Y'.
caution: The たとえ must be answered by 〜ても/〜でも later in the sentence; dropping the ても (leaving bare たとえ…) is the classic error — たとえ only sets up the concessive, it can't stand alone.

examples.en: Even if it rains, I'll go to the interview.
Even if the pay is low, I want to keep this job.
Even if my Japanese is poor, I'll keep on speaking.

peg.ja: たとえ倒れても、俺は前に進む。
peg.romaji: Tatoe taorete mo, ore wa mae ni susumu.
peg.en: Even if I fall, I'll keep moving forward.
peg.source: 僕のヒーローアカデミア — Deku (styled)

nuance: Highlights an extreme or minimal example — 'even this much'.
caution: さえ (extreme example 'even') is different from さえ…ば (sole condition 'as long as'); also さえ replaces を/が but stacks after other particles (name さえ書けない), so don't leave を in (×名前をさえ).

examples.en: I was so tired I couldn't even write my own name.
I'm so busy I don't even have time to drink water.
My part-time job is so busy I can't even study Japanese.

peg.ja: お前の名前さえ、俺は忘れない。
peg.romaji: Omae no namae sae, ore wa wasurenai.
peg.en: I won't forget even your name.

nuance: Just this one condition is enough for the result.
caution: さえ…ば says just this one condition suffices — the verb must be in ば/なら form, and with nouns it's typically N さえ あれば, not N さえ です; don't confuse this 'if only' with plain 'even' さえ.

examples.en: As long as I have the money, I can live in this share house.
As long as I have time, I'll study every day.
As long as I know the password, I can use the internet.

peg.ja: 君さえいれば、他には何もいらない。
peg.romaji: Kimi sae ireba, hoka ni wa nani mo iranai.
peg.en: As long as I have you, I need nothing else.

nuance: Two things change together, gradually and in step.
caution: につれて needs a gradual, linked change on BOTH sides (as one rises, the other rises) — it can't take a one-off event or a deliberate action, and unlike と/たら it doesn't express a simple condition.

examples.en: As summer approaches, the humidity increases.
As I got used to Japanese, daily life became easier.
As time went by, I got used to life in Tokyo.

peg.ja: 強くなるにつれて、孤独も深くなる。
peg.romaji: Tsuyoku naru ni tsurete, kodoku mo fukaku naru.
peg.en: As you grow stronger, your solitude deepens too.

nuance: Increasing the first proportionally increases the second.
caution: The verb must repeat in ば…ほど form (考えれば考えるほど); な-adjectives take なら…なほど and い-adjectives ければ…いほど — a common slip is writing the verb only once or mismatching the two halves.

examples.en: The more I think about it, the less I understand.
The more you practice, the better you get.
The more you walk around Tokyo, the more new shops you discover.

peg.ja: 戦えば戦うほど、俺は強くなる。
peg.romaji: Tatakaeba tatakau hodo, ore wa tsuyoku naru.
peg.en: The more I fight, the stronger I get.

nuance: Nothing else reaches that degree — the ultimate example.
caution: ほど…はない is a superlative ('nothing is as … as X'), so X is the top of the scale — don't confuse it with ほど meaning 'about/to the extent'; the はない negative is essential and can't be dropped.

examples.en: Nothing is as tiring as a packed train.
There's no season as gloomy as the rainy season.
There's no mountain as beautiful as Mt. Fuji.

peg.ja: 仲間ほど大切なものはない。
peg.romaji: Nakama hodo taisetsu na mono wa nai.
peg.en: There's nothing as precious as my crew.

meaning: to the extent that / so … that
nuance: Gives a vivid measure of how extreme something is.
caution: This 'to the extent that' くらい (degree) is a different sense from くらい 'approximately' — here it illustrates how extreme something is via a vivid comparison, so it attaches to a clause, not just a quantity.

examples.en: I walked so much that my legs hurt.
I was so happy I could have cried.
I was so hungry I could have collapsed.

peg.ja: 死ぬくらい、修行はきつかった。
peg.romaji: Shinu kurai, shugyō wa kitsukatta.
peg.en: The training was so brutal it nearly killed me.

nuance: Downplays: nothing in particular, no strong feeling either way.
caution: 別に requires a following negative — 別に used with a positive (×別に行きたい) is wrong; and a curt '別に。' alone reads as sulky/dismissive, so mind the tone.

examples.en: I don't particularly want to go.
This izakaya isn't particularly expensive.
This neighborhood isn't particularly inconvenient.

peg.ja: 別に、君のためじゃないんだからね。
peg.romaji: Betsu ni, kimi no tame ja nai n dakara ne.
peg.en: It's not particularly for your sake, okay?

nuance: Adds a second, often bigger, item beyond the first.
caution: だけでなく usually pairs with も on the second, bigger item (A だけでなく B も) — forgetting that も, or treating B as smaller than A, is the classic slip; often reinforced as だけでなく…も.

examples.en: This konbini sells not only food but also daily goods.
He can speak not only English but also Japanese.
In Tokyo, not only the trains but the buses too are convenient.

peg.ja: 力だけでなく、心も鍛えろ。
peg.romaji: Chikara dake de naku, kokoro mo kitaero.
peg.en: Train not only your strength but your heart.

nuance: Since the situation is fixed, a natural obligation follows — 'now that X, of course Y'.
caution: This 以上 ('now that X, of course Y') attaches to a verb/clause and demands a fitting obligation, resolve, or expectation in the second half — don't confuse it with 以上 'more than' (quantity) or the letter-closing 以上.

examples.en: Now that I've promised, I'll see it through to the end.
Since I work in Japan, I have to pay taxes.
Now that I've signed the contract, I have to pay the rent.

peg.ja: 戦うと決めた以上、後悔はしない。
peg.romaji: Tatakau to kimeta ijō, kōkai wa shinai.
peg.en: Now that I've decided to fight, I won't regret it.

connection: plain form / N-の + くせに
nuance: Like のに but scornful — implies the subject should know or do better.
caution: くせに is openly scornful, so it's rude aimed at superiors and can't describe yourself (×私は…くせに); the subject of both clauses must be the same person, unlike neutral のに.

examples.en: Even though he's a newbie, he acts so cocky.
Even though he doesn't know the way, he tries to lead.
Even though he promised, he didn't come.

peg.ja: 弱いくせに、口だけは達者だな。
peg.romaji: Yowai kuse ni, kuchi dake wa tassha da na.
peg.en: You're weak, yet all you've got is a big mouth.

connection: plain form / N-の + わりに
nuance: The result is unexpected given the standard you'd assume.
caution: わりに means 'unexpectedly, given the standard you'd assume from X' — it's not plain contrast のに; the two clauses must share a scale (small room → yet high rent), and it lacks the scorn of くせに.

examples.en: For how small it is, this room's rent is high.
For how young he is, he's very composed.
For how cheap it is, this place gives big portions.

peg.ja: 子供のわりに、なかなか肝が据わっている。
peg.romaji: Kodomo no wari ni, nakanaka kimo ga suwatte iru.
peg.en: For a kid, you've got quite some nerve.

connection: plain form / N-の + おかげで
nuance: Credits a good outcome to a cause — grateful tone.
caution: おかげで credits a GOOD result, so swapping it with せいで (which blames a bad result) is the classic mix-up — sarcastic 'おかげで' for a bad outcome exists but is deliberately ironic, not the default.

examples.en: Thanks to my coworker, I got used to the work.
Thanks to this study book, I passed the exam.
Thanks to my senpai, I got used to the job.

peg.ja: お前のおかげで、俺は立ち上がれた。
peg.romaji: Omae no okage de, ore wa tachiagareta.
peg.en: Thanks to you, I was able to stand up again.
peg.source: 僕のヒーローアカデミア — Deku (styled)

connection: plain form / N-の + せいで
nuance: Blames a bad outcome on a cause — the opposite of おかげで.
caution: せいで blames a BAD result on a cause, the mirror of おかげで (positive) — using せいで for something good, or おかげで for something bad, is the stock error; せい also nominalizes as せいだ/せいか.

examples.en: Because I overslept, I missed the train.
Because of the typhoon, the flight was cancelled.
Because I overslept, I missed the train.

peg.ja: 油断したせいで、敵に逃げられた。
peg.romaji: Yudan shita sei de, teki ni nigerareta.
peg.en: Because I let my guard down, the enemy got away.

connection: plain form + からといって
nuance: Rejects a too-easy conclusion: X being true doesn't automatically give Y.
caution: からといって rejects a hasty inference, so the sentence normally ends in a negative like わけではない/とは限らない — leaving it with a plain affirmative loses the 'doesn't necessarily mean' force.

examples.en: Just because I live in Tokyo doesn't mean I know all the streets.
Just because it's cheap doesn't mean the quality is bad.
Just because it's a sale doesn't mean you should overbuy.

peg.ja: 強いからといって、勝てるとは限らない。
peg.romaji: Tsuyoi kara to itte, kateru to wa kagiranai.
peg.en: Just because you're strong doesn't mean you'll win.

nuance: Introduces a term to define or explain it.
caution: というのは introduces a term to define OR gives a reason ('the reason is…'); don't confuse the defining use with plain topic は — というのは signals an explanation is coming, so it needs a following account.

examples.en: 'Shikikin' means the security deposit you pay up front.
A 'zairyu card' is a foreigner's ID.
'Shikikin' means the money that comes back to you when you move out.

peg.ja: 友達というのは、そばにいてくれる者のことだ。
peg.romaji: Tomodachi to iu no wa, soba ni ite kureru mono no koto da.
peg.en: A friend is someone who stays by your side.

nuance: Wraps a whole clause into a noun — the fact/idea that ...
caution: ということ nominalizes a whole clause (the fact/that…), distinct from こと alone or という 'called'; watch ということだ, which at sentence end can mean hearsay 'I hear that…', not just 'it means that…'.

examples.en: I heard the fact that he's quitting.
The fact that rent is going up is a big deal.
Working in Japan means paying taxes.

peg.ja: 生きるということは、戦い続けるということだ。
peg.romaji: Ikiru to iu koto wa, tatakai tsuzukeru to iu koto da.
peg.en: To live is to keep on fighting.

nuance: Reassures: there's no need to do that; don't bother.
caution: ことはない here means 'no need to' (reassurance), attaching to V-dict — don't confuse it with past 〜たことがない 'have never'; the point is 'you don't have to', not 'has never happened'.

examples.en: There's no need to hurry; there's time until the last train.
There's no need to worry about it alone.
There's no need to worry all by yourself.

peg.ja: 泣くことはない、まだ終わっていない。
peg.romaji: Naku koto wa nai, mada owatte inai.
peg.en: There's no need to cry; it's not over yet.

nuance: Draws a natural conclusion from a fact — 'so that's the reason'.
caution: わけだ draws a logical 'so that's why/no wonder' conclusion from given facts — it's not わけではない (partial denial) nor わけにはいかない (social can't); using わけだ where you mean 'I decided to' (that's ことにする) is a stock error.

examples.en: So the heater's broken. No wonder it's cold.
He's been in Tokyo ten years. That's why he knows the streets.
The AC was left on the whole time — no wonder the electricity bill is high.

peg.ja: なるほど、それで負けたわけだ。
peg.romaji: Naruhodo, sore de maketa wake da.
peg.en: I see — so that's why you lost.

connection: plain form + わけではない
nuance: Partial denial: it's not the case that ... entirely.
caution: わけではない is a PARTIAL denial ('it's not that…entirely'), not a flat negation — don't read it as 'definitely not'; contrast it with わけがない ('there's no way', strong denial) and plain わけだ (conclusion).

examples.en: It's not that I dislike Japanese food.
It doesn't mean I work overtime every single day.
It's not that I can't speak Japanese at all.

peg.ja: 勝てないわけではないが、油断は禁物だ。
peg.romaji: Katenai wake de wa nai ga, yudan wa kinmotsu da.
peg.en: It's not that we can't win, but we mustn't be careless.

connection: V-dict + わけにはいかない
nuance: Can't do it for social or moral reasons, even if physically possible.
caution: わけにはいかない means can't for social/moral reasons though physically possible — don't confuse it with わけだ or わけではない; its negative-verb form (〜ないわけにはいかない) flips to 'have no choice but to do'.

examples.en: I have an interview tomorrow, so I can't very well skip it.
I can't very well turn down my boss's invitation.
The interview's tomorrow, so I can't very well skip work.

peg.ja: ここで逃げるわけにはいかない。
peg.romaji: Koko de nigeru wake ni wa ikanai.
peg.en: I can't very well run away here.

nuance: Confident logical denial — there's no way that's true.
caution: はずがない is a confident logical 'can't possibly be', based on reasoning — don't confuse it with はずだ 'should be (expected)' or わけがない (near-synonym but blunter); it denies likelihood, not permission.

examples.en: Someone as diligent as him can't possibly be late.
There's no way a shop is open at this hour.
I practiced so hard, there's no way I'll fail.

peg.ja: あいつが負けるはずがない。
peg.romaji: Aitsu ga makeru hazu ga nai.
peg.en: There's no way he'd lose.

connection: plain form / N + に違いない
nuance: Strong conviction based on evidence — surely that's it.
caution: に違いない is the speaker's strong personal conviction from evidence ('surely'), not objective certainty — for reported/hearsay use そうだ/らしい instead; it attaches directly to nouns without な/だ (×学生だに違いない).

examples.en: The streets are wet. It must have rained overnight.
He must be from Hokkaido, no doubt.
The lights are on, so someone must be home.

peg.ja: この気配、あいつに違いない。
peg.romaji: Kono kehai, aitsu ni chigainai.
peg.en: This presence — it must be him.

nuance: Exactly as specified — following the model, plan, or instruction.
caution: 通りに means 'exactly as (specified/shown)'; after a verb it reads とおり (言ったとおりに), after a noun it's の通り/どおり with rendaku (予定どおり) — mixing up the とおり/どおり reading is the usual slip.

examples.en: I assembled it just as the manual said.
I transferred at the station just as my senior told me.
When I walked just as the map showed, I reached the station.

peg.ja: 計画通り。
peg.romaji: Keikaku-dōri.
peg.en: Just as planned.
peg.source: デスノート (Death Note) — 夜神月

nuance: The volitional + とする marks an attempt or the moment right before an action begins — often a try that fails or a state on the verge of happening.
caution: ようとする marks an attempt or the verge of acting, so it takes the volitional stem (食べようとする), not V-dict + とする; and ようとしない ('refuses to') is a fixed nuance, not just 'doesn't try'.

examples.en: Just as I was about to board the last train, the doors closed.
I try to speak in Japanese, but the words just won't come out.
I tried to go to sleep early, but I couldn't.

peg.ja: 人を理解しようとする。
peg.romaji: Hito o rikai shiyō to suru.
peg.en: I'll try to understand people.
peg.source: 葬送のフリーレン — Frieren (styled)

nuance: V-stem + かけ describes something started but left partway through — a half-drunk coffee, an unfinished sentence.
caution: かけ/かける means an action left half-done (読みかけの本); don't confuse it with 〜かける 'begin to' onset — context decides, and '死にかける' means 'nearly die', a special sense, not 'half-die'.

examples.en: I left my half-drunk coffee on the desk and headed out.
He started to say something, then suddenly went quiet.
I set my half-finished coffee on the desk.

peg.ja: やりかけたことは、最後までやる。
peg.romaji: Yarikaketa koto wa, saigo made yaru.
peg.en: What I've started, I finish to the end.

nuance: がち marks an unwanted tendency — something that happens all too often, usually with a negative flavor.
caution: がち marks an unwanted, frequent TENDENCY and is usually negative (病気がち, 遅れがち) — don't use it for good habits, and don't confuse it with ぎみ 'a slight touch of', which is milder and more momentary.

examples.en: In winter I tend to catch colds.
After a night shift, I tend to skip breakfast.
In winter I tend to shut myself in at home.

peg.ja: 一人だと、暗く考えがちになる。
peg.romaji: Hitori da to, kuraku kangaegachi ni naru.
peg.en: When I'm alone, I tend to think dark thoughts.

nuance: 気味 (rendaku ぎ) adds 'a slight sense of' — a mild, noticeable tendency toward the noun or state.
caution: ぎみ (気味) means 'a slight touch of' a state (風邪ぎみ, 疲れぎみ) and is milder/more temporary than がち — reserve it for a light onset you feel, not a repeated habitual tendency.

examples.en: Lately I've been a bit worn out from all the overtime.
I feel a cold coming on, so I'll head home early today.
Lately I've been feeling a touch tired.

peg.ja: 最近、少し疲れ気味だ。
peg.romaji: Saikin, sukoshi tsukaregimi da.
peg.en: Lately I've been a bit worn out.
peg.source: エヴァンゲリオン — Shinji (styled)

nuance: っぽい means '-like' or 'has a strong quality of' — childish, forgetful, reddish. Often carries a mildly critical tone.
caution: っぽい means '-ish/has a strong quality of' and is colloquial and often mildly critical (子供っぽい, 忘れっぽい) — don't confuse it with らしい 'typical/befitting' (男らしい = manly-good vs 男っぽい = mannish).

examples.en: This sweater looks a bit childish.
He's forgetful, so I check with him again and again.
That shop clerk is a bit quick-tempered.

peg.ja: お前、意外と子供っぽいな。
peg.romaji: Omae, igai to kodomoppoi na.
peg.en: You're surprisingly childish.

nuance: 続ける attaches to a verb stem to mean 'continue doing' — the action goes on without stopping.
caution: 続ける attaches to the masu-stem (降り続ける) and means the actor keeps doing it — for a state that merely stays ongoing use 続く (intransitive); mixing 続ける (do continuously) with 続く (continue on its own) is the classic error.

examples.en: I keep studying Japanese every morning.
The train wouldn't come, so I kept waiting at the station.
I'll keep studying Japanese without giving up.

peg.ja: 諦めずに、走り続けろ。
peg.romaji: Akiramezu ni, hashiri tsuzukero.
peg.en: Don't give up — keep on running.

nuance: 出す on a verb stem marks a sudden, often unexpected onset of an action — 'burst out ...ing'.
caution: 出す on a stem marks a SUDDEN onset ('burst out …ing', 泣き出す) with no control implied — it can't take a volitional/intended start; for a deliberate or gradual beginning use 始める instead.

examples.en: The sky suddenly darkened and it started to rain.
The baby next to me suddenly burst out crying.
The moment the train started moving, I realized I'd forgotten something.

peg.ja: ベルが鳴ると、彼は走り出した。
peg.romaji: Beru ga naru to, kare wa hashiridashita.
peg.en: When the bell rang, he broke into a run.

nuance: 切る on a verb stem means carrying an action fully through to completion — finish off, use up entirely.
caution: 切る on a stem means fully completing/exhausting an action (使い切る, 食べ切る) — don't take it literally as 'cut'; the negative 切れない often means 'can't finish/too many to', not 'can't cut'.

examples.en: I read the whole long novel through in one night.
I managed to run the marathon all the way to the finish.
I blew through my whole first paycheck in a single day.

peg.ja: 最後まで、この力を使い切る。
peg.romaji: Saigo made, kono chikara o tsukaikiru.
peg.en: I'll use up this power to the very end.

nuance: 合う on a verb stem expresses a reciprocal or mutual action — doing something to or with each other.
caution: 合う on a stem means a MUTUAL/reciprocal action done to each other (助け合う, 話し合う) — it needs a plural or 'with each other' sense; don't confuse it with 合う 'to match/fit' as an independent verb.

examples.en: I split the housework with the other share-house residents.
When we're in trouble, my friends and I help each other out.
Before the interview, my friend and I cheered each other on.

peg.ja: 仲間と支え合って、ここまで来た。
peg.romaji: Nakama to sasae atte, koko made kita.
peg.en: We supported each other and made it this far.

nuance: 直す on a verb stem means doing the action again, usually to correct or improve it — 'redo', 'do over'.
caution: 直す on a stem means redo to correct/improve (書き直す) — don't confuse it with the auxiliary meaning of just 'again'; and 直す as its own verb 'to fix/cure' is separate, so context tells 'redo' from 'repair'.

examples.en: I made a mistake, so I rewrote the document.
My pronunciation was off, so I said it over again in front of the teacher.
I rewrote my resume over and over.

peg.ja: 何度でも、立ち直してみせる。
peg.romaji: Nando demo, tachinaoshite miseru.
peg.en: No matter how many times, I'll pick myself back up.
peg.source: 僕のヒーローアカデミア — Deku (styled)

nuance: The humblest way to say 'I will do' — literally 'receive the favor of being allowed to do', common in service and workplace keigo.
caution: させていただく is humble and presumes the listener grants permission, so it's odd for things needing no permission (over-used, it sounds obsequious); build it from the causative せる/させる — 休ませていただく, not ×休みさせていただく.

examples.en: Today, I will be the one to show you around (humbly).
Might I be allowed to leave a little early tomorrow?
If it's all right, I'll take my leave a bit early today.

peg.ja: 本日は、私が案内させていただきます。
peg.romaji: Honjitsu wa, watashi ga annai sasete itadakimasu.
peg.en: Today, I shall humbly show you the way.

nuance: The passive れる・られる form doubles as a light honorific — a softer way to elevate someone's action than お〜になる.
caution: The honorific れる/られる is identical in form to the passive and the potential, so context must disambiguate (社長が来られた = 'came' honorific, not 'was come'); it's lighter respect than お〜になる and never used about yourself.

examples.en: Has the store manager already gone home? (honorific)
The president reads the newspaper every morning. (honorific)
The president has already gone home.

peg.ja: 会長はもう帰られました。
peg.romaji: Kaichō wa mō kaeraremashita.
peg.en: The president has already gone home.
peg.source: かぐや様は告らせたい — Hayasaka (styled)

connection: V-ない stem + ずに (する→せずに)
nuance: A more formal, written equivalent of 〜ないで meaning 'without doing'. Note the irregular する → せずに.
caution: ずに = ないで 'without doing' but more written; the irregular is する → せずに (never ×しずに), and 来る → 来 (こ)ずに — using しずに for する is the stock mistake.

examples.en: I went to work without eating breakfast.
Don't worry about anything and just leave it to me.
I left home without eating breakfast.

peg.ja: 振り返らずに、前を向いて歩け。
peg.romaji: Furikaerazu ni, mae o muite aruke.
peg.en: Walk forward without looking back.

nuance: A non-contiguous pattern: なかなか paired with a negative predicate means something just won't happen despite effort or expectation.
caution: In this pattern なかなか REQUIRES a negative predicate and means 'won't … easily / not for a while' (なかなか来ない); with a positive verb なかなか instead means 'quite/considerably', so the polarity flips the meaning.

examples.en: The bus just won't come.
Kanji are hard, and I just can't seem to memorize them.
In Tokyo, a cheap apartment is hard to find.

peg.ja: 答えが、なかなか見つからない。
peg.romaji: Kotae ga, nakanaka mitsukaranai.
peg.en: The answer just won't come easily.

nuance: Dictionary form + ところだった describes something that nearly happened but was avoided — 'I almost ...'.
caution: V-dict + ところだった means something ALMOST happened but didn't (危うく…ところだった) — don't confuse it with V-た ところだ 'just finished' or V-ている ところ 'in the middle of'; the tense of the verb before ところ changes everything.

examples.en: I almost missed the last train.
I very nearly dropped my wallet.
I almost missed the last train.

peg.ja: 危なかった、もう少しで死ぬところだった。
peg.romaji: Abunakatta, mō sukoshi de shinu tokoro datta.
peg.en: That was close — I nearly died.

nuance: て-form + ばかりいる criticizes someone for doing only one thing all the time and nothing else.
caution: てばかりいる criticizes doing only one thing to the exclusion of all else (遊んでばかりいる) — it attaches to the て-form, unlike ばかり after a noun ('only N'); the tone is reproachful, so avoid it about yourself neutrally.

examples.en: My little brother does nothing but play video games at home.
He does nothing but complain and never actually does anything.
My roommate does nothing but complain.

peg.ja: 泣いてばかりいると、前に進めないぞ。
peg.romaji: Naite bakari iru to, mae ni susumenai zo.
peg.en: If you do nothing but cry, you can't move forward.

nuance: て-form + はじめて marks the point where something first became true or understood — 'only after ... did I ...'.
caution: てはじめて marks the moment something first became true/understood ('only after …'), and the main clause is usually a realization (気づいた/分かった) — don't read はじめて here as the plain adverb 'for the first time' detached from the て-clause.

examples.en: Only after living alone did I understand how much my parents did for me.
It was only after coming to Japan that I first tried natto.
Only after living on my own did I realize how much my parents did for me.

peg.ja: 失って初めて、その大切さに気づいた。
peg.romaji: Ushinatte hajimete, sono taisetsusa ni kizuita.
peg.en: Only after losing it did I realize how precious it was.

nuance: ように言う reports an indirect request or instruction — 'tell someone to (not) do something'.
caution: ように言う reports an indirect request/instruction; the embedded verb is plain (行くように言う = tell to go), and a negative request uses ないように言う (tell NOT to do) — dropping ように and just quoting is the usual slip.

examples.en: The doctor told me to cut back on alcohol.
The manager told me not to be late.
The doctor told me to go easy on alcohol.

peg.ja: 無理をしないように、と伝えてくれ。
peg.romaji: Muri o shinai yō ni, to tsutaete kure.
peg.en: Tell him not to overdo it.

connection: V-て + いただけませんか
nuance: A very polite request: て-form + いただけませんか literally asks 'could I not receive the favor of you doing ...?' — the softest 'could you please'.
caution: ていただけませんか is a very polite request built on the humble いただく — keep it in the negative-question form (…ませんか); saying ×ていただきませんか or mixing in もらえる (which is casual) breaks the register.

examples.en: Excuse me, could you kindly take a photo for me?
Could you kindly explain it to me one more time?
Excuse me, could you kindly take a photo for me?

peg.ja: 少し待っていただけませんか。
peg.romaji: Sukoshi matte itadakemasen ka.
peg.en: Could you kindly wait a moment?
peg.source: ヴァイオレット・エヴァーガーデン — Violet (styled)

nuance: について marks the topic a statement, question, or research is *about* — neutral and common in reports and study.
caution: について marks the TOPIC something is about (研究する対象) — don't confuse it with に対して 'toward/against a target'; について is the subject-matter, not the direction of an attitude or action.

examples.en: I asked about my visa at the ward office.
I'm studying about Japanese culture.
I'm looking into Japanese taxes.

peg.ja: その事件について、話がある。
peg.romaji: Sono jiken ni tsuite, hanashi ga aru.
peg.en: There's something I want to discuss about that case.

nuance: に対して directs an attitude or action *toward* a target, or sets one thing *in contrast* against another.
caution: に対して directs an action/attitude TOWARD a target or sets up a contrast — don't swap it with について (mere topic): 'Xについて怒る' is wrong for 'angry at X'; anger is directed, so it needs に対して.

examples.en: The store manager is always polite toward customers.
My brother likes meat. In contrast, I like fish.
Toward customers, you use polite language.

peg.ja: 強き者に対して、決して媚びない。
peg.romaji: Tsuyoki mono ni taishite, kesshite kobinai.
peg.en: I'll never grovel before the powerful.

nuance: にとって frames a judgment from a person or group’s standpoint — "for X, this is …".
caution: にとって frames a value judgment from someone's STANDPOINT ('for X, this is important') — don't confuse it with に 'to' (recipient) or として 'as a role'; it needs an evaluative predicate (大切/necessary), not a plain action.

examples.en: For a foreign student, rent is expensive.
For me, Tokyo summers are too hot.
For a foreigner, kanji is difficult.

peg.ja: 君にとって、俺は何だ？
peg.romaji: Kimi ni totte, ore wa nan da?
peg.en: What am I to you?

nuance: によって covers three jobs: the agent in a passive ("done by"), variation ("depending on"), and cause ("due to").
caution: によって juggles three senses — agent-in-passive ('done by'), 'depending on', and 'due to' — so read the clause to pick the right one; for a pure passive agent, low-animacy causes prefer によって over に.

examples.en: The price differs depending on the shop.
The trains stopped due to the typhoon.
Depending on the season, Tokyo's scenery changes.

peg.ja: 努力によって、運命は変えられる。
peg.romaji: Doryoku ni yotte, unmei wa kaerareru.
peg.en: Through effort, fate can be changed.

nuance: によると cites a source of information; the sentence usually ends in a hearsay そうだ / らしい.
caution: によると cites an information source, so the sentence should END in hearsay (そうだ/らしい/ということだ) — pairing によると with a flat assertion, or confusing it with によって 'by means of', is the classic error.

examples.en: According to the forecast, it will rain tomorrow.
According to my friend, that izakaya is cheap.
According to the forecast, it'll snow tomorrow.

peg.ja: 報告によると、敵艦が接近している。
peg.romaji: Hōkoku ni yoru to, tekikan ga sekkin shite iru.
peg.en: According to the report, the enemy ship is approaching.
peg.source: 機動戦士ガンダム — Bright (styled)

nuance: として states the role, capacity, or category in which someone or something acts.
caution: として states the ROLE/capacity in which someone acts ('as a student') — don't confuse it with にとって 'from the standpoint of': 'as a teacher I decide' is 教師として, while 'for a teacher this is hard' is 教師にとって.

examples.en: I work as an interpreter.
I'm using this room as a bedroom.
As a working holiday worker, I'm working in Tokyo.

peg.ja: 軍人として、命令には従う。
peg.romaji: Gunjin to shite, meirei ni wa shitagau.
peg.en: As a soldier, I follow orders.

nuance: 代わりに swaps one thing for another, or offsets one act with a return act ("in exchange").
caution: 代わりに has two senses — substitution ('instead of') and compensation ('in exchange') — so context decides; after a noun it's Nの代わりに, after a verb V-dict + 代わりに, and forgetting the の with nouns is a common slip.

examples.en: I paid with an IC card instead of cash.
I'll clean. In exchange, you cook.
Instead of cash, I paid with my IC card.

peg.ja: 肉の代わりに魚を出そう。
peg.romaji: Niku no kawari ni sakana wo dasou.
peg.en: Instead of meat, I'll serve fish.

nuance: はもちろん takes one obvious item for granted, then adds a second — "A of course, and even B".
caution: はもちろん takes the first item as OBVIOUS and adds a second, usually with も (Aはもちろん B も) — the second item should be the more surprising one; dropping the も or reversing which item is 'obvious' weakens it.

examples.en: This shop is crowded in the daytime, not to mention at night.
He can speak Japanese, let alone English.
Weekends of course, but weekdays are packed too.

peg.ja: 勝利はもちろん、全員無事に帰る。
peg.romaji: Shōri wa mochiron, zen'in buji ni kaeru.
peg.en: Victory of course, but we all get home safe too.

nuance: なんか cites an example casually, often with a dismissive or self-deprecating tone ("stuff like…", "someone like me").
caution: なんか is casual and often dismissive/self-deprecating (私なんか…), so it's inappropriate in formal speech — its polite/written counterpart is など; using なんか up the hierarchy can sound rude or falsely modest.

examples.en: On my day off I don't want to do stuff like cleaning.
Someone like me still can't read kanji.
I'm tired today, so I don't feel like cooking or anything.

peg.ja: 涙なんか、見せたくなかった。
peg.romaji: Namida nanka, misetakunakatta.
peg.en: I didn't want to show any tears or anything.

nuance: こそ singles out its word for emphasis — "this one, precisely" — often to correct or to reciprocate ("こちらこそ").
caution: こそ emphasizes 'this one precisely' and replaces は/が (今度こそ, こちらこそ) — don't stack it with those particles (×はこそ); it stresses the preceding word, so put it right after the item you're singling out.

examples.en: This year, precisely, I want to go to Hokkaido.

ja: ありがとうございます
examples.en: The thanks are mine — thank *you*.
This year for sure, I want to pass the N3.

peg.ja: 今度こそ、必ず君を守る。
peg.romaji: Kondo koso, kanarazu kimi o mamoru.
peg.en: This time for sure, I'll protect you.
peg.source: 美少女戦士セーラームーン — Tuxedo Mask (styled)

nuance: だって is a casual even/too — "even N" or "N too". Lists split it between N3 and N2, so treat the level as unsettled.
caution: だって is casual for 'even/too' (でも/も) and also a colloquial 'because…' at sentence head — don't use it in formal writing; and sentence-initial だって (excuse-making 'but…') is a different, whiny nuance.

examples.en: Even a child can read that kanji.
I want to go to the izakaya too, you know.
Even I want to treat myself once in a while.

peg.ja: 俺だって、強くなりたいんだ。
peg.romaji: Ore datte, tsuyoku naritai n da.
peg.en: I want to get strong too, you know.

meaning: casual quotative / topic "you said / speaking of"
nuance: って is casual と/という — it quotes ("X said …"), names ("a thing called X"), or raises a topic ("speaking of X").
caution: って is casual と/という — quotative, naming, or topic-marker — strictly spoken; it's not for formal writing, and topic って ('speaking of X') shouldn't be confused with the quotative って ('X said').

examples.en: The manager said tomorrow is a day off.
Share houses — are they cheap?
Tanaka-san is really kind, isn't she.

peg.ja: 友情って、案外いいもんだな。
peg.romaji: Yūjō tte, angai ii mon da na.
peg.en: This friendship thing is surprisingly nice.

nuance: 向け marks the intended audience a thing was *made for* ("designed for beginners"), unlike 向き which is mere suitability.
caution: 向け marks the intended TARGET audience a thing was deliberately made for (子供向け = made for kids), unlike 向き which is mere suitability (子供向き = suits kids) — swapping 向け and 向き is the classic N3 trap.

examples.en: This is a map made for foreigners.
I'm looking for a Japanese class aimed at beginners.
This is a Japanese book aimed at beginners.

peg.ja: これは初心者向けの訓練だ。
peg.romaji: Kore wa shoshinsha muke no kunren da.
peg.en: This is training meant for beginners.
peg.source: 僕のヒーローアカデミア — All Might (styled)

nuance: 向き describes natural suitability ("suited for beginners", "facing south") — a fit, not a deliberate target like 向け.
caution: 向き describes natural SUITABILITY or orientation (初心者向き, 南向き), not deliberate design — for 'made intentionally for' use 向け; also 向き can mean 'facing (a direction)', a sense 向け never has.

examples.en: This room faces south and is bright.
This job is suited for students.
This job is well suited for beginners.

peg.ja: お前は、リーダー向きの男だ。
peg.romaji: Omae wa, rīdā muki no otoko da.
peg.en: You're the type suited to be a leader.

nuance: 的 turns a noun into a な-adjective ("economic", "personal") — な before nouns, に as an adverb.
caution: 的 makes a な-adjective, so it's 〜的な before a noun and 〜的に as an adverb (経済的な問題 / 経済的に) — attaching 的 to native-Japanese or already-adjectival words, or dropping the な/に, is the usual error.

examples.en: Personally, I like this shop.
I'm moving for economic reasons.
Japanese people are basically kind.

peg.ja: 基本的に、俺は諦めが悪い。
peg.romaji: Kihonteki ni, ore wa akirame ga warui.
peg.en: Basically, I'm terrible at giving up.

nuance: さ turns an adjective into a noun measuring its degree — 高い → 高さ (height), 便利 → 便利さ (convenience).
caution: さ nominalizes to measure DEGREE objectively (高さ = height); use さ on the adjective stem (高い→高さ, 便利→便利さ) — don't confuse it with み, which gives a subjective/experiential noun (暖かみ vs 暖かさ).

examples.en: I was surprised by the heat of Tokyo summers.
I've gotten used to the convenience of the trains.
I was shocked by the intensity of Tokyo's summer heat.

peg.ja: その強さの秘密を、教えてくれ。
peg.romaji: Sono tsuyosa no himitsu o, oshiete kure.
peg.en: Tell me the secret of that strength.

connection: N + 中 (家中, 一日中)
nuance: 中 read じゅう means "all through" a whole span or space (一日中 = all day, 家中 = the whole house) — distinct from ちゅう "in the middle of".
caution: 中 read じゅう means 'throughout' a whole span/space (一日中, 世界中), while ちゅう means 'in the middle of / during' (授業中) — picking the wrong reading flips 'all day' into 'while it's daytime'.

examples.en: I studied in my room all day.
The cat ran all over the house.
I spent the whole day preparing for the interview.

peg.ja: 一日中、剣を振り続けた。
peg.romaji: Ichinichijū, ken o furitsuzuketa.
peg.en: I kept swinging my sword all day long.

nuance: ごとに means "at each interval" — 一駅ごとに (every station), 会うごとに (each time we meet).
caution: ごとに means 'at each interval' and can take a number (三日ごとに = every 3 days), unlike たびに which needs an event — also 一つおきに 'every OTHER' is different from ごとに 'every', a frequent mix-up.

examples.en: This train stops at every station.
I take out the burnable garbage every three days.
Every year, I renew the contract.

peg.ja: 季節ごとに、景色は姿を変える。
peg.romaji: Kisetsu goto ni, keshiki wa sugata o kaeru.
peg.en: With each season, the scenery changes its form.

nuance: はずだった states an expectation that failed to happen — "it was supposed to …, but …".
caution: はずだった states an expectation that FAILED ('was supposed to, but…'), so a contrary result should follow — don't confuse it with はずだ 'should be' (still expected) or はずがない 'can't possibly be'.

examples.en: I was supposed to meet my friend today, but it was called off because of rain.
The train was supposed to arrive at nine.
The package was supposed to arrive today, but it still hasn't come.

peg.ja: 今日で、全て終わるはずだった。
peg.romaji: Kyō de, subete owaru hazu datta.
peg.en: Today, everything was supposed to end.

connection: V-dict + べき(だ) (する→すべき)
nuance: べき states a strong should/ought — a duty or the right thing to do. With する it becomes すべき (or するべき).
caution: べき is a strong 'should/ought' (moral duty), not mere advice (that's ほうがいい); with する it's すべき (or するべき, never ×すべく here), and べきだった means 'should have (but didn't)' — a past regret, so don't use plain べき for past obligations.

examples.en: You should go to the ward office before moving.
You ought to start saving money now.
If you're not feeling well, you shouldn't push yourself.

peg.ja: 守るべきものが、俺にはある。
peg.romaji: Mamoru beki mono ga, ore ni wa aru.
peg.en: I have something I must protect.

nuance: に比べて sets a baseline for comparison — "compared to X, Y is …".
caution: に比べて sets X as the BASELINE you compare against ('compared to X, Y is…') — don't confuse it with より (simple 'than' in comparisons); 比べて needs an explicit reference point, and the judgment goes on Y, not X.

examples.en: Compared to last year, rent has risen.
Compared to Tokyo, Hokkaido is cool.
Compared to last year, my Japanese has improved a lot.

peg.ja: あの頃に比べて、俺は強くなった。
peg.romaji: Ano koro ni kurabete, ore wa tsuyoku natta.
peg.en: Compared to back then, I've grown stronger.
